summary: Markets extended their six-week rally on Friday, with the Nasdaq hitting fresh records near 26,247 as semiconductor stocks surged over 60% year-to-date on AI infrastructure spending. The S&P 500 closed at 7,399, up 0.84%, marking its longest winning streak since 2024. Bitcoin ETFs pulled in over $1 billion this week—the first billion-dollar week since January—pushing BTC above $81,000 as institutional demand accelerates. The April jobs report beat expectations with 115,000 additions versus 65,000 forecast, signaling labor market resilience despite Iran conflict uncertainties. The Fed held rates steady at its April 29 meeting in an unprecedented 8-4 split decision, with Powell announcing he will remain as a governor after his chairmanship ends May 15 when Kevin Warsh takes over. March CPI came in hot at 3.3% year-over-year, driven by a 21.2% spike in gasoline from Middle East tensions. Mega-cap earnings season delivered beats from Apple, Amazon, Alphabet, and AMD, with Q1 S&P 500 earnings up 21.2% on strong AI capex and cloud growth, though oil above $95 and sticky core inflation at 2.6% keep pressure on margins.

signals.title: Semiconductor rally extends as Philadelphia SOX Index surges 60% YTD on AI infrastructure buildout
signals.desc: AMD crossed $700 billion market cap after beating Q1 earnings, while Micron and NVIDIA post strong gains as hyperscaler capex accelerates, fueling chip orders despite geopolitical risks.

signals.title: Bitcoin ETFs record first $1 billion weekly inflow since January; BlackRock's IBIT dominates
signals.desc: Spot Bitcoin ETFs pulled $1.63 billion in the first week of May, with $2.44 billion in April—the strongest monthly figure since October 2025—signaling renewed institutional appetite.

signals.title: Fed holds rates at 3.5-3.75% in historic 8-4 split decision as Powell's tenure ends May 15
signals.desc: Four FOMC members dissented in April meeting; committee forecasts one rate cut in December 2026 amid uncertainty from Iran conflict and elevated energy-driven inflation at 3.3%.

signals.title: April jobs report beats with 115,000 additions vs 65,000 expected; unemployment holds at 4.3%
signals.desc: Labor market shows resilience despite zero private-sector job creation over six months per Powell; wage growth of 3.5% continues to outpace inflation, supporting consumer spending.

signals.title: Michael Burry warns AI rally resembles dot-com bubble final stages as valuations stretch
signals.desc: Burry compared semiconductor index trajectory to 1999-2000 run-up; March CPI surged 0.9% monthly with core at 2.6%, keeping Fed on hold and pressuring rate-cut expectations into 2027.

verdict: Tech and crypto lead risk-on rally as AI spending trumps inflation fears, but sticky CPI and Fed leadership transition cloud rate-cut timeline.
